result.chapter_summary: Chapter 7 argues that a multiethnic nation requires a common educational foundation to function as a single 'people.' By examining the historical intentions of America's founders and the sociolinguistic nature of national communication, the author asserts that shared knowledge and language serve as the essential 'intellectual currency' that overrides racial and regional divisions.

claims.text: Shared learned habits, references, symbols, and memories permit more effective communication within a nation than shared language alone.
claims.source_passage: The Swiss may speak four languages and still act as one people, for each of them has enough learned habits, references, symbols, memories, patterns of landholding and social stratification, events in history, and personal associations, all of which together permit him to communicate more effectively with other Swiss than with the speakers of his own language who belong to other peoples.

claims.text: Shared speech patterns and language can override racial prejudice by signaling 'sameness' to the brain's cognitive processing.
claims.source_passage: If the person who doesn’t look like you speaks like you, your brain short-circuits because your racism program has none of those instructions in the code. “Wait, wait,” your mind says, “the racism code says if he doesn’t look like me he isn’t like me, but the language code says if he speaks like me he . . . is like me?”

claims.text: The United States and Prussia were the first modern societies to implement free public schooling nationwide.
claims.source_passage: The United States of America was the first big modern democracy, and along with Prussia the first society with free public schooling nationwide.

claims.text: Noah Webster's primary social objective was the creation of a unified 'people' through the instrument of schooling.
claims.source_passage: It was Noah Webster’s social aim from the start to create a “people” through schooling.

claims.text: The United States was the first modern nation to introduce public schools where children of all social ranks studied the same curriculum.
claims.source_passage: If you look up world histories of education, you will find that the United States was the first in the modern era to introduce public schools at which children “of all ranks of society” would study the same things.

claims.text: The early American preference for common educational content was a direct result of an urgent desire for national unity.
claims.source_passage: Our early preference for common content was part of our urgent desire for national unity.

claims.text: For the first 150 years of its existence, the United States successfully created a united citizenry through common language and common schooling.
claims.source_passage: In the first century and a half of our national existence, we succeeded in creating a united citizenry based on a common language and common schooling.

claims.text: The American founders believed internal factions—based on ethnicity, region, religion, and class—posed a mortal danger to the republic.
claims.source_passage: Benjamin Franklin and Benjamin Rush from Pennsylvania, and Madison, Jefferson, and George Washington from Virginia believed that a mortal danger lay in our potential internal conflicts—Germans against English inside Pennsylvania, state against state, region against region, local interests against national interests, party against party, personal ambition against personal ambition, religion against religion, poor against rich, uneducated against educated.

claims.text: Constitutional checks and balances are insufficient to preserve a republic without a citizenry that subordinates local interests to the common good.
claims.source_passage: To keep that from happening, they believed we would need far more than the checks and balances described in the Constitution. We would also need a special new brand of citizen who, unlike the citizens of Rome and other failed republics, would subordinate his local interests to the common good.

claims.text: Schools were intended to be the primary institution for transforming future citizens into loyal Americans by teaching common knowledge and virtues.
claims.source_passage: Our early educators thought the school would be the institution that would transform future citizens into loyal Americans. It would teach common knowledge, virtues, ideals, language, and commitments.

claims.text: Benjamin Rush advocated for a common elementary curriculum for all to create active and purposeful citizens.
claims.source_passage: Benjamin Rush, a signer of the Declaration of Independence, wrote one of the most important early essays on American education. It advocated a common elementary curriculum for all.

claims.text: George Washington intended for education to spread systematic ideas across the nation to dissolve local attachments and state prejudices.
claims.source_passage: Washington bequeathed a portion of his estate to education in order (he wrote) “to spread systematic ideas through all parts of this rising Empire, thereby to do away local attachments and State prejudices.”

claims.text: Thomas Jefferson’s plan for common schools was designed to ensure social fairness by providing equal educational opportunity to children regardless of their parents' wealth.
claims.source_passage: Thomas Jefferson’s plan for common schools every five miles aimed not only to secure the peace and safety of the republic but also to engender social fairness and to foster the best leaders. He outlined a system of elementary schooling that enabled all children, rich and poor, to go to the same school so that they would get an equal chance regardless of who their parents happened to be

claims.text: Nature distributes talent equally among the poor and the rich.
claims.source_passage: we hope to avail the state of those talents which nature has sown as liberally among the poor as the rich.

claims.text: The primary aim of early American common schools was to assimilate both immigrants and native-born Americans from different regions into a shared language and a common American identity.
claims.source_passage: The aim was not just to assimilate the many immigrants then pouring into the nation but also to assimilate native-born Americans who came from different regions and social strata into the common language and the common American idea.

claims.text: The 19th-century common school curriculum was intentionally designed to foster patriotism and solidarity among students.
claims.source_passage: the early grades were to have a common core curriculum that would foster patriotism and solidarity.

claims.text: Providing common textbooks to all students was a deliberate strategy to create a shared base of knowledge in subjects like history, grammar, and arithmetic.
claims.source_passage: As early as 1825, the New York legislature established a fund to secure common textbooks for all of the state’s elementary schools, specifying that the printing of large editions of such elementary works as the spelling book, an English dictionary, a grammar, a system of arithmetic, American history and biography, to be used in schools, and to be distributed gratuitously, or sold at cost.

claims.text: The goal of early American education was not political or religious indoctrination but the development of independent citizens capable of self-governance.
claims.source_passage: The aim, they said, was not to make our children and youth either partisans in politics, or sectarians in religion; but to give them education, intelligence, sound principles, good moral habits, and a free and independent spirit; in short, to make them American free men and American citizens, and to qualify them to judge and choose for themselves in matters of politics, religion and government.

claims.text: The maintenance of a democratic republic depends on an education that combines mental enlightenment with the teaching of civic duty.
claims.source_passage: It cannot be doubted that in the United States the education of the people powerfully contributes to the maintenance of the democratic republic. That will always be so, in my view, wherever education to enlighten the mind is not separated from the education of civic duty.

claims.text: American education in the 19th century was more successful than European education because it directed students toward public political life rather than private life.
claims.source_passage: In the United States the general thrust of education is directed toward political life; in Europe its main aim is to fit men for private life.

claims.text: Early American schools were effective at counteracting the natural defects of democracy and human nature.
claims.source_passage: I speedily discovered that the Americans had made great and successful efforts to counteract these imperfections of human nature and to correct the natural defects of democracy. Such was the success of our school leaders and teachers in those cautious early days.

claims.text: Primers and spelling books were the primary technology for reinforcing nation-creating on a massive scale.
claims.source_passage: Nation creating was reinforced in our primers and spelling books on a scale never before seen in human history.

claims.text: Reverence for secular laws and democracy was intended to serve as a 'civil religion' that could unify people across different sectarian faiths.
claims.source_passage: The new doctrine was no longer to be sectarian religion but devotion to democracy and reverence for its laws. The old religions were to be made less lethal by making them subject to civil law. Later in the nineteenth century, Lincoln wanted his patriotic, secular “reverence for the laws” to be the new civil religion.

claims.text: The American political experiment requires a vigorous public sphere, created by schools and media, to function correctly while respecting private autonomy.
claims.source_passage: Like Madison and Lincoln, they understood that the American political experiment, which respected everyone’s autonomy in the private sphere, depended on a vigorous public sphere that only the schools and newspapers could create.

claims.text: Without the shared public sphere created by common schools, a republic is at risk of collapsing into a military dictatorship.
claims.source_passage: Those early speeches are filled with cautionary references to the South American republics that were then collapsing into military dictatorships. Unless our schools created Americans, they warned, that would be our fate.

claims.text: The American political experiment depends on a vigorous public sphere that only schools and newspapers can create.
claims.source_passage: Like Madison and Lincoln, they understood that the American political experiment, which respected everyone’s autonomy in the private sphere, depended on a vigorous public sphere that only the schools and newspapers could create.

claims.text: Unless schools create a unified American identity, the nation risks collapsing into military dictatorship.
claims.source_passage: Unless our schools created Americans, they warned, that would be our fate [collapsing into military dictatorships].

claims.text: The success or failure of self-government is wholly dependent on the cultivation of the minds of the young in schools.
claims.source_passage: On the careful cultivation in our schools, of the minds of the young, the entire success or the absolute failure of the great experiment of self-government is wholly dependent

claims.text: The American Union must be sustained by the moral and intellectual powers of the community rather than physical force.
claims.source_passage: That Union is and must be sustained by the moral and intellectual powers of the community, and every other power is wholly ineffectual. Physical force may generate hatred, fear and repulsion; but can never produce Union.

claims.text: The decline of patriotism in the modern United States is palpable.
claims.source_passage: The decline of patriotism is palpable.

claims.text: American teacher-training institutes are dominated by the tradition of child-centered progressivism.
claims.source_passage: Earlier, I described how our schools persist in the blithe tradition of child-centered progressivism, which took over the thought world of our teacher-training institutes.

claims.text: The decline of shared knowledge and ideals leads directly to political polarization.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, the decline of shared knowledge and ideals leads to factions and to incompetence—or as we say these days, to “polarization.”

claims.text: The abandonment of shared knowledge has led to a 'dumbing-down' of the American population according to objective criteria like PISA results.
claims.source_passage: And—as we don’t like to admit—it leads to a dumbing-down of our people, according to objective criteria like the PISA results.

claims.text: Overconfidence in national success during the 1930s and 1940s caused the United States to abandon the idea of educational commonality.
claims.source_passage: By the 1930s and 1940s, our great success as a nation had made us overconfident and overly optimistic. We no longer worried about the precariousness of republican government.

claims.text: The biological nature of humans provides a blank slate for culture to write upon rather than a developmental blueprint.
claims.source_passage: As we now know, nature does not want us humans to take its “providential course,” because it has given us a blank slate rather than a blueprint.

claims.text: True language proficiency is an expertise that functions as a prerequisite for political office and college admission.
claims.source_passage: True proficiency of language is an expertise that every single American deserves to have—a proficiency that opens up all sorts of opportunities that are otherwise closed, including an opportunity to run for political office.

claims.text: A shared language cannot exist without shared background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: And shared language requires shared knowledge.

claims.text: Increasing commonality in elementary schools would improve American life without creating conformist robots.
claims.source_passage: A renewed increase of commonality in American elementary schools would make American life better and more productive, and do so without turning people into conformist robots.

claims.text: Shared language and knowledge enable individual competence rather than suppressing individuality.
claims.source_passage: shared language and knowledge doesn’t suppress individuality; rather, it enables greater competence.

claims.text: Political success in the United States requires the ability to use and understand the common language of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: to be nominated or elected who doesn’t talk the talk and understand the talk.

claims.text: Acceptance to high-quality colleges is contingent on performing well on the verbal sections of standardized entrance exams.
claims.source_passage: Nobody is going to be accepted at a good college who doesn’t do well on the verbal section of a college entrance exam.

claims.text: Even poorly educated Americans desire to understand public discourse and expect speakers to use formal language.
claims.source_passage: In the public sphere, even the most poorly educated Americans want to understand what is being said. They want their public speakers to use the formal language of radio, television, and the speaker’s lectern.

claims.text: Increasing educational commonality improves national productivity without enforcing robot-like conformity.
claims.source_passage: A renewed increase of commonality in American elementary schools would make American life better and more productive, and do so without turning people into conformist robots.

claims.text: Every American child deserves full mastery of Standard American English regardless of their geographic location.
claims.source_passage: No matter what state or city an American child lives in, he or she deserves to gain full mastery of Standard American English.

claims.text: Standard American English is a neutral tool for the public sphere and is not the property of any specific social group.
claims.source_passage: It has no special group attached to it. It is the language that announces: “I’m just as good as you. And you are just as good as I am. We are equal.”

claims.text: Silently shared knowledge is the decisive feature of a shared national identity.
claims.source_passage: Also essential to language competence is silently shared knowledge that may not appear on the surface but is the decisive feature of a shared identity.

claims.text: Integrating diverse cultural perspectives into the school curriculum is an easy and desirable goal.
claims.source_passage: There is an understandable demand to include in the school curriculum more representation of African American and Hispanic culture and people... This is an easy and desirable demand to put into effect.

claims.text: Knowledge must be shared across the community to be functional in language and culture.
claims.source_passage: Knowledge is not functional in language and culture until it is shared.

claims.text: The primary practical challenge for education is determining which specific minority-related and traditional elements of knowledge should be shared grade-by-grade.
claims.source_passage: Hence the key practical matter to settle is determining which are the specific elements of knowledge about African Americans, Hispanics, and other minorities that need to be shared going forward.

claims.text: Current American pedagogy views the requirement of specific grade-by-grade topics in humanities and arts as untouchable or 'poisonous'.
claims.source_passage: In the current pedagogy of American elementary education, the requirement of specific topics is a nearly untouchable concept except in the uncontroversial subject of mathematics. Trying to get nationwide or statewide or even district or schoolwide agreement about specific grade-by-grade subject matter in history, literature, or the arts is like touching some poisonous object.

claims.text: Educational non-commonality actively disadvantages poor children and results in national mediocrity.
claims.source_passage: But there are worse things than being criticized, if as a consequence of noncommonality poor children become even more disadvantaged and one’s country performs at a mediocre level.

claims.text: The education profession is too entrenched in its opposition to commonality to solve the curriculum problem from within.
claims.source_passage: The solution now lies mainly with the public; opposition seems too entrenched among many of our education professionals.

claims.text: The public should pressure education officials by making their jobs contingent on implementing a common curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The public should make the need for a common curriculum more threatening to the jobs of controversy-averse education officials than any conceivable gripe about a choice of particular content.

claims.text: Democracies have sound practical reasons to focus on the well-being of their specific nation while still engaging in international cooperation.
claims.source_passage: But there are sound practical reasons for democracies to focus their attention on the well-being of their nation while still engaged in cooperative internationalism.

claims.text: The nation-state is the specific social arrangement that is most effective at achieving goals and getting things done.
claims.source_passage: the nation-state is the social arrangement that gets things done.

claims.text: International groupings like the European Union have not replaced the nation-state and are not destined to do so.
claims.source_passage: But so far, the evidence is that international groupings like the European Union have not replaced the nation-state, and they do not seem destined to do so.

claims.text: The modern nation-state will remain the ultimate limit of human social organization for a very long time.
claims.source_passage: The evidence indicates that the modern nation-state will remain for a very long time the ultimate limit of human social organization

claims.text: Society is the fundamental precondition for the existence of civilization and humanity.
claims.source_passage: It must be maintained that society is the very precondition of civilization and humanity.

claims.text: The nation is the most highly organized form of society.
claims.source_passage: And since the nation is nothing other than the most highly organized society, you can see that to deny the nation is not simply to deny her received ideas; it is to deny the moral life at its very source.

claims.text: It is an error to believe that humanity in general can be counterposed to or replace the fatherland (the nation).
claims.source_passage: No doubt, we believe that we can counterpoise the fatherland with humanity in general. That is a huge mistake.

claims.text: Multilingual empires, such as the Soviet Union, are inherently unstable societies.
claims.source_passage: the most recent empire in history is a textbook case for the unstable society of the multilingual empire—the Soviet Union.

claims.text: Standardized national print languages and their associated cultures have proven to be stubbornly persistent even under suppression.
claims.source_passage: The post-Gutenberg era of standardized national print languages has revealed those print languages and national cultures to be stubbornly persistent.

claims.text: Plural languages within a single nation do not automatically make nationwide social unity impossible.
claims.source_passage: Switzerland, though small, had already shown that plural languages alone do not make nationwide social unity impossible.

claims.text: The Soviet Union failed as a society because it lacked unity of shared knowledge and values and relied on coercion.
claims.source_passage: The difficulty in the Soviet Union was that, unlike Switzerland, it lacked a unity of shared knowledge and value. Quite the contrary: the Soviet Union was patched together under coercion without shared knowledge and values.

claims.text: The European Union does not qualify as a 'society' because it contains significant disjunctions in shared knowledge and values.
claims.source_passage: Similarly, the European Union... is nowhere near what Durkheim would call a 'society.' There are enormous shared-knowledge-and-value disjunctions within the European Union

claims.text: Globalism lacks the cultural and emotional unity required to ensure permanence.
claims.source_passage: In short, globalism, the idea that 'our one world is one nation,' lacks the cultural and emotional unity that could promise permanence.

claims.text: Shared, value-laden knowledge is the essential underpinning of unity in any society.
claims.source_passage: teach shared, value-laden knowledge, which is, as Durkheim and Deutsch wisely say, the essential underpinning of unity in any society.

claims.text: American elementary schools must teach shared knowledge to solve the problem of national disunity.
claims.source_passage: how our elementary schools might once again be brought to teach shared, value-laden knowledge, which is... the essential underpinning of unity in any society.

claims.text: The exclusion of women from the historical discourse on American citizen-making is a valid and significant contemporary grievance.
claims.source_passage: Such was the success of our school leaders and teachers in those cautious early days. (Of course. the exclusion of women from these comments rankles today.)

claims.text: The architects of South African apartheid strategically utilized language barriers as a tool for social and physical segregation among Black people.
claims.source_passage: The architects of apartheid understood this. Part of the effort to divide black people was to make sure we were separated not just physically but by language as well.

claims.text: In a successful multiethnic nation like Switzerland, citizens communicate more effectively with co-nationals of different languages than they do with foreigners who speak their native tongue.
claims.source_passage: ...all of which together permit him to communicate more effectively with other Swiss than with the speakers of his own language who belong to other peoples.

claims.text: Racism operates as a cognitive 'program' or 'code' that is disrupted when a person's physical appearance and linguistic patterns are inconsistent.
claims.source_passage: If the person who doesn’t look like you speaks like you, your brain short-circuits because your racism program has none of those instructions in the code.

claims.text: The public has a moral obligation to support educational officials who demonstrate the courage to implement specific, grade-by-grade content requirements.
claims.source_passage: Then, if members of the public do raise their voices, they also have an obligation to support any official who has the courage to require grade-by-grade specificity of school topics.

claims.text: The transition to progressive education in the 1930s involved a shift from viewing nature as 'red in tooth and claw' to viewing it as a 'providential' guide for development.
claims.source_passage: In the 1930s we ceased thinking of nature as red in tooth and claw, and we lost our founders’ acute sense of tragedy and danger.

claims.text: The American founders' approach to government and education was characterized by a 'knowledge-drenched caution' informed by the failure of ancient republics.
claims.source_passage: It wants us to act as our American founders did, with a knowledge-drenched caution, based on the sad history of earlier republics.

claims.text: Denying the nation-state as the primary unit of social organization effectively denies the source of moral life itself.
claims.source_passage: And since the nation is nothing other than the most highly organized society, you can see that to deny the nation is not simply to deny her received ideas; it is to deny the moral life at its very source.

evidence.description: Karl W. Deutsch's 1953 work on nationalism, arguing that shared social communication (habits, memories) creates national cohesion even across language barriers.
evidence.source_reference: Karl W. Deutsch, Nationalism and Social Communication (1953)

evidence.description: Trevor Noah's autobiographical account of how shared language overrides the visual cues of race in social interactions.
The historical naming of American infrastructure (Union Avenue, Union Square) as evidence of the priority of national unity.
Benjamin Rush's early essay on American education advocating for a common curriculum to create 'republican machines.'
evidence.source_reference: Benjamin Rush, 'Thoughts upon the Mode of Education Proper in a Republic' (1786)

evidence.description: George Washington's will and estate bequest for education to promote national ideas over local attachments.
evidence.source_reference: George Washington, Last Will and Testament

evidence.description: Thomas Jefferson's legislative plan for common schools and scholarship systems for the poor.
New York State Common School Act of 1812.
The 1825 New York legislative fund for common textbooks (English dictionary, spelling books, American history, etc.).
Alexis de Tocqueville's observations in 'Democracy in America' (1835) regarding the effectiveness of American education in fostering civic duty.
evidence.source_reference: Alexis de Tocqueville, Democracy in America (1835)

evidence.description: Speeches by New York state education superintendents and Governor Silas Wright (1845).
The historical collapse of South American republics into dictatorships during the 19th century as a negative contrast to the US.
Historical speeches by 19th-century governors and state superintendents warning of national collapse without unified schooling.
evidence.source_reference: Speeches of state education superintendents and governors (early-mid 19th century)

evidence.description: The 1845 address by Governor Silas Wright to the New York legislature regarding schools as the salvation of the republic.
evidence.source_reference: Silas Wright, Address to the New York legislature, 1845

evidence.description: Current political polling showing American dissatisfaction with the country's direction.
International assessment data showing a decline or stagnation in American student performance.
A 1940s film featuring John Dewey explaining that education should not prepare children for the current world but for their future world.
The requirement of verbal proficiency for political and academic success in the U.S.
The historical failure of the European Union to supersede the nation-state structure.
Karl Deutsch's observation in his work on nationalism regarding the functionality of the nation-state.
evidence.source_reference: Karl Deutsch, Nationalism and Social Communication

evidence.description: Emile Durkheim's 1910 lecture asserting the nation-state as the limit of human organization.
Karl Deutsch's observation that the nation-state remains the primary mechanism for social action.
evidence.source_reference: Karl Deutsch, Nationalism and Social Communication (Second Edition)

evidence.description: Emile Durkheim's 1910 lecture stating that the nation is the highest organized society and source of moral life.
The failure of the Soviet Union to suppress or 'swallow' the distinct print languages and cultures of Ukraine, Latvia, and Lithuania.
The ability of Swiss citizens to defend cantons where different languages are spoken, illustrating unity despite linguistic diversity.

concepts.author_definition: Active, loyal, and purposeful citizens formed by a common curriculum to serve the interests of the republic.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes interpreted as a dehumanizing term for robotic citizens; Hirsch emphasizes it as 'purposeful' and 'loyal.'
concepts.source_passage: The paramount aim of the schools, he wrote, was to create “republican machines.” By that he meant active, loyal, purposeful citizens of the republic.

concepts.author_definition: A social and political state where individual and local interests are subordinated to a shared national character and common good.
The shared educational foundation (knowledge, language, values) that welds diverse individuals into a single people.
A taxpayer-supported elementary school with a common, statewide administration and curriculum intended to educate all children together regardless of social class.
The educational aim of welding diverse individuals into a single people with a shared allegiance to the republic and its laws.
A secular devotion to democracy and patriotic 'reverence for the laws' that replaces or subordinates sectarian religious differences to a shared civic identity.
A vigorous, shared space of communication and common knowledge, created by schools and newspapers, where diverse groups meet as equals.

The progressive idea that society flourishes if nature is allowed to take its course in the child; characterized by Hirsch as a fallacy because humans have a blank slate, not a developmental blueprint.
The modern term for 'factions' and incompetence caused by a decline in shared knowledge and ideals.
Shared knowledge that becomes part of an individual's identity, analogous to an immigrant earning citizenship and belonging to the whole.
The language of the public sphere that transcends group identity and serves as a tool for perceived and actual equality and competence.
A prevailing American cultural value that emphasizes individual uniqueness but often mistakenly views shared knowledge as a threat to that individuality.
A focus on the well-being and commonality of a nation-state as a practical social arrangement for getting things done, distinct from 'white nationalism' or xenophobia.
The most highly organized political grouping (the nation) that serves as the precondition for civilization, humanity, and moral life.
The idea that the whole world should be treated as a single nation, which Hirsch argues fails because it lacks emotional and cultural unity.
The common intellectual and ethical currency that provides the underpinning for societal unity, beyond just technical or economic ties.
A historical and scientific awareness of the fragility of republics and the 'imperfections of human nature' that informs prudent governance and education.
The optimistic, progressive belief that child development and society will naturally progress toward improvement if left to follow their natural path.
A metaphorical cognitive program that dictates social expectations based on physical appearance, specifically the assumption that difference in appearance implies difference in identity.
A view of nature as a site of struggle, tragedy, and danger, which the author contrasts with modern educational optimism.

cases.name: Prussia
cases.description: Identified alongside the US as one of the first societies to establish free, nationwide public schooling.
key_passages: The United States of America was the first big modern democracy, and along with Prussia the first society with free public schooling nationwide.

cases.name: Early American Schooling (Founding-19th Century)
cases.description: The deliberate use of common schools to overcome fractional interests (class, ethnicity, religion) and create a unified American citizenry.
key_passages: In the first century and a half of our national existence, we succeeded in creating a united citizenry based on a common language and common schooling.
Our early preference for common content was part of our urgent desire for national unity.

cases.name: New York State Common School System
cases.description: Beginning in 1812, New York established a statewide system of public elementary schools and later (1825) created a fund for common textbooks. The state leaders were specifically motivated by the need to integrate a diverse population of immigrants and regional native-born Americans into a shared public sphere.
key_passages: As early as 1812, New York State passed the Common School Act, providing the basis for a statewide system of public elementary schools.
As early as 1825, the New York legislature established a fund to secure common textbooks for all of the state’s elementary schools

New York State, with its diversity of immigrants and religious affiliations, was especially alert to the need to build up a shared public sphere where all these different groups could meet as equals on common ground.

cases.name: 19th Century South American Republics
cases.description: The author cites the collapse of South American republics into military dictatorships as a cautionary tale used by 19th-century American leaders. This case served as a warning that without schools to 'create Americans,' the US political experiment would fail.
key_passages: Those early speeches are filled with cautionary references to the South American republics that were then collapsing into military dictatorships. Unless our schools created Americans, they warned, that would be our fate.

cases.name: Mid-19th Century New York
cases.description: Faced with diversity from immigration and religion, New York's leaders emphasized that a unified school system was necessary to create 'Americans' and maintain the public sphere required for self-government.
key_passages: New York State, with its diversity of immigrants and religious affiliations, was especially alert to the need to build up a shared public sphere where all these different groups could meet as equals on common ground.

cases.name: The European Union
cases.description: Cited as an example of an international grouping that attempted to move beyond the nation-state but failed to replace it as the primary unit of social organization.
key_passages: the evidence is that international groupings like the European Union have not replaced the nation-state, and they do not seem destined to do so.

cases.name: The Soviet Union
cases.description: An example of a multilingual empire that attempted to unify disparate groups through coercion and common currency but failed because it lacked shared knowledge and values and could not suppress the print languages of member states like Ukraine.
key_passages: In fact, the most recent empire in history is a textbook case for the unstable society of the multilingual empire—the Soviet Union.
It had its common currency, but it could not swallow Ukraine, much less Latvia and Lithuania, each with an established print language perpetuated in elementary schools.
the Soviet Union was patched together under coercion without shared knowledge and values.

cases.name: Switzerland
cases.description: A successful multilingual nation that achieves unity not through a single language, but through shared knowledge, values, and a common devotion to the homeland that transcends linguistic boundaries.
key_passages: Switzerland, though small, had already shown that plural languages alone do not make nationwide social unity impossible.

in Switzerland, many an individual Swiss is a member of an army whose members stand ready to die in defense of their homeland even if the attacking force has invaded a canton they do not live in, and one whose inhabitants speak a language they do not.

thinkers.name: Karl W. Deutsch
thinkers.key_idea: National identity is built on social communication patterns rather than just language.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Deutsch's quote as an epigraph to establish the theoretical basis for why shared references (background knowledge) are required for a multiethnic nation to function.
key_passages: The Swiss may speak four languages and still act as one people, for each of them has enough learned habits, references, symbols, memories... which together permit him to communicate more effectively with other Swiss than with the speakers of his own language who belong to other peoples.

thinkers.name: Trevor Noah
thinkers.key_idea: Language functions as a 'code' that can short-circuit the 'racism program' by establishing commonality.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Noah's experience in apartheid South Africa to argue that shared language is a powerful tool for overcoming racial division.
key_passages: If the person who doesn’t look like you speaks like you, your brain short-circuits because your racism program has none of those instructions in the code.

thinkers.name: Benjamin Rush
thinkers.key_idea: Schools should produce 'republican machines' through a common elementary curriculum.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Rush as a primary historical authority for the common school tradition's goal of national cohesion.
key_passages: The paramount aim of the schools, he wrote, was to create “republican machines.” By that he meant active, loyal, purposeful citizens of the republic.

thinkers.name: Thomas Jefferson
thinkers.key_idea: Education should identify and educate 'youths of genius' from the poor to ensure social fairness.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch frames Jefferson's educational plan as a tool for meritocracy and social equity within the common school framework.
key_passages: Our plan prescribes the selection of the youths of genius from among the classes of the poor; we hope to avail the state of those talents which nature has sown as liberally among the poor as the rich.

thinkers.name: Alexis de Tocqueville
thinkers.key_idea: The success of American democracy depends on education that links enlightenment of the mind with civic duty.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Tocqueville as an 'astute foreign observer' to prove that the early American schools' aims were actually being met in practice.
key_passages: It cannot be doubted that in the United States the education of the people powerfully contributes to the maintenance of the democratic republic. That will always be so, in my view, wherever education to enlighten the mind is not separated from the education of civic duty.

thinkers.name: Abraham Lincoln
thinkers.key_idea: A patriotic, secular 'reverence for the laws' should serve as a new civil religion.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch links Lincoln's idea of 'civil religion' to the mission of the common schools as 'secular pulpits.'
key_passages: Later in the nineteenth century, Lincoln wanted his patriotic, secular “reverence for the laws” to be the new civil religion.

thinkers.name: Silas Wright
thinkers.key_idea: The salvation of the republic depends entirely on the cultivation of young minds in schools; intellectual power, not force, sustains the Union.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch explicitly states that Wright's 1845 premise is 'precisely correct' and central to the book's thesis.
key_passages: The only salvation for the republic is to be sought for in our schools.
It’s the premise of this book that Silas Wright, like Noah Webster, was precisely correct.

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: Education should follow nature and prepare children for their future world rather than the current one.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Dewey as an emblem of the overoptimistic progressive shift in the 1930s/40s that abandoned commonality for a flawed 'follow nature' philosophy.
key_passages: The footage of John Dewey telling the audience that we are not preparing our children for our world but theirs is especially emblematic.
Dewey was a highly sophisticated philosopher, but also a believer in the premise that to follow nature is to progress onward and upward.

thinkers.name: Émile Durkheim
thinkers.key_idea: The modern nation-state is the ultimate limit of human social organization.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Durkheim to provide historical sociological support for the permanence and importance of the nation-state.
key_passages: the modern nation-state will remain for a very long time the ultimate limit of human social organization, as the sociologist Émile Durkheim stated in a 1910 lecture

actors.name: Noah Webster
actors.context: Early American educator who aimed to create a 'people' through unified schooling.

actors.name: Horace Mann
actors.context: Praised globally as an early exponent of the modern national school.

actors.name: George Washington
actors.context: Founder who used his estate to promote 'systematic ideas' to eliminate regional prejudices.

actors.name: Daniel Webster
actors.context: Statesman quoted to show the priority of the 'Union' in American political oratory.

actors.name: George Washington
actors.context: Cited for his intent to use education to unify the 'rising Empire' and eliminate local prejudices.

actors.name: New York State Legislature
actors.context: Cited for establishing common textbooks and school systems to manage diversity.

actors.name: Silas Wright
actors.context: Governor of New York who warned that the republic depended on schools creating a shared identity.

actors.name: Silas Wright
actors.context: 1845 Governor of New York who argued schools are the salvation of the republic.

actors.name: John Dewey
actors.context: Philosopher and face of progressive, child-centered education.

actors.name: Teacher-training institutes
actors.context: Organizations that Hirsch claims have been indoctrinated with progressive, child-centered ideas.

actors.name: Education Professionals
actors.context: Described as entrenched opponents of a common curriculum and grade-by-grade specificity.

actors.name: The Public / Parents
actors.context: Urged to take political action against education officials to force curriculum changes.

actors.name: Karl Deutsch
actors.context: Expert on nationalism and a founder of the UN.

actors.name: Émile Durkheim
actors.context: Sociologist whose 1910 lecture defines the nation as the pinnacle of social organization.

actors.name: European Union
actors.context: Used as a contemporary example of an international grouping that fails to become a true 'society'.

objections_raised.objection: Early American educational thinkers and observers like Tocqueville spoke only of men and excluded women from the civic sphere.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch concedes this 'rankles today' but maintains that their success in nation-building through commonality was nonetheless historically significant.

objections_raised.objection: A common-core curriculum and shared knowledge would turn children into conformist robots and suppress their individuality.
objections_raised.response: Shared language and knowledge do not suppress individuality; instead, they enable greater competence and allow individuals to function successfully in the public sphere.

objections_raised.objection: Nationalism is inherently negative and associated with xenophobia or white nationalism.
objections_raised.response: There are sound practical reasons for democracies to focus on national well-being as the nation-state is the social arrangement that gets things done.

objections_raised.objection: Demands for diversity in curriculum conflict with the idea of a common curriculum.
objections_raised.response: Diversity is easy to include; the goal is to determine which specific elements of minority history and culture should be shared by all students as part of the common curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: The focus on national commonality is a form of 'deplorable white nationalism.'
objections_raised.response: Nationalism is practically necessary for democracy to function and 'get things done,' and it provides the essential moral framework for society that internationalism cannot replace.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: Earlier, I described how our schools persist in the blithe tradition of child-centered progressivism
